title: Camera-Equipped AirPods Pro Development 'Suspended,' Leaker Claims
content:
Development of Apple's rumored camera-equipped AirPods Pro has been halted, according to the leaker and prototype collector known as "Kosutami."


In a blunt post on X referencing a June post about the product "case" being "concluded," the leaker appeared to be correcting it to say that the project has been "suspended." No other details were provided.

Bloomberg reported in May that the camera-equipped AirPods Pro were nearing completion and had reached an "advanced" testing stage, suggesting early mass production could be imminent. 

That said, the same report also noted that Apple could also opt to wait to launch the AirPods if it was not satisfied with the quality of the ‌Visual Intelligence‌ features in development for ‌Siri‌.

February that the infrared cameras will allow for the AirPods Pro to be "connected with Apple Intelligence," and other reliable sources have since corroborated that claim. The new AirPods have been in development for around four years.

Apple reportedly wanted to start selling the camera-equipped AirPods Pro in the first half of 2026, but the product's launch was held back because the smarter, AI version of ‌Siri‌ was still being developed – it exists in the current iOS 27 beta, but it won't be widely available until the release of iOS 27 in September.

Bloomberg said Apple was expecting strong demand for the new AirPods Pro and had operations teams working to secure components for a launch in challenging circumstances, owing to the industry-wide shortage of memory chips and other silicon. It's not clear whether this is the reason behind Kosutami's claim that the project has been suspended.

metal-enclosed battery. 

early look at an Apple Watch band made from Apple's controversial FineWoven material. However, Kosutami incorrectly claimed that AirPods Pro 3 were imminent in August 2024.

title: Mophie StealthCharge review: Wireless charging without the heat
content:
Macworld



At a glance
Expert's Rating



Pros
3-in-1 for iPhone, Apple Watch, and AirPods
60W retractable USB-C cable (4-in-1)
Fast 25W for iPhone (16 and later)
Apple Watch fast charge
Adjustable viewing angles
Includes 40W/100W charger
Cons
Expensive compared with rivals
25W limited to iPhone 16+
Our Verdict 
Mophie’s clever StealthCharge technology enables this multi-device charger to deliver a cooler, faster, and quieter charging experience than found in most other Apple device chargers. The retractable cable in the 4-in-1 model means you shouldn’t need another charger even for your MacBook.
Price When Reviewed
This value will show the geolocated pricing text for product undefined
Best Pricing Today


Price When Reviewed
$179.95
Retailer
				
Price
				
$179.95						
View Deal						
Product
				
Price
				

								
							
						
What is it? Mophie’s StealthCharge is a new wireless charging design that moves heat-generating electronics from the charging pad into the stand’s base, keeping your iPhone cooler while maintaining full charging speeds. Unlike most MagSafe chargers, it reduces heat without noisy cooling fans, resulting in faster, quieter and more consistent wireless charging.
Who is it for? Apple users wanting fast, cooler multi-device charging with minimal desk clutter, especially iPhone 16 owners and anyone charging a MacBook too.
Mophie’s StealthCharge is a new wireless charging design that moves heat-generating electronics from the charging pad into the stand’s base, keeping your iPhone cooler while maintaining full charging speeds. Unlike most MagSafe chargers, it reduces heat without noisy cooling fans, resulting in faster, quieter and more consistent wireless charging.
Wireless charging is so fast these days that it’s catching up with wired cable charging. The only thing holding wireless charging back from truly catching up with wired cable power is the inefficiency of heat that can force the device being charged to slow the charger down when it gets too hot for the phone’s good.
Mophie, a pioneer in phone charging since the beginning, has come up with a novel solution that’s so simple it seems strange that it’s not commonplace. It calls its new technology StealthCharge, and its first products are a range of multi-device chargers: the 3-in-1 and 4-in-1 Wireless Charge Stands and another folding travel charger called Roam.
Why wireless charging usually generates heat
Wireless charging is convenient because it eliminates the need to find the right cable when your phone needs a boost of power. It has improved rapidly with Apple’s MagSafe technology that corrected charging coil misalignment between phone and charger. MagSafe and its third-party Qi2 and Qi 2.2 offspring have progressed the coil alignment to reduce the magnetic field not transferring energy (electromagnetic induction) efficiently, which otherwise can generate heat.
Temperature is a battery’s nemesis. To protect its battery, your phone’s thermal management system automatically throttles charging speeds when the heat risks damaging it. This throttling slows down charging.
To reduce this heat problem and maintain a steady charge, some manufacturers install fans near the charging pads, but this can create unwanted noise as the fans struggle to reduce the heat.

				
					

				
			
Simon Jary




How StealthCharge reduces heat
Mophie’s solution is clever because, like many of the best challenge beaters, it’s simple. I’m sure the engineering isn’t simple, but the idea is.
StealthCharge moves the hottest components from the charging pad itself (in direct contact with the phone) to the product’s base. In effect, the base now serves as a heat sink to dissipate heat more efficiently. The power is then sent to the charging pad via the stand’s stem.
StealthCharge relocates the following components:
Integrated controller circuitry, including the 25W Qi2.2 controller integrated circuit
Power Delivery (PD) negotiation integrated circuit
DC-to-DC buck-boost integrated circuit: a power management chip that can both step-down (buck) and step-up (boost) input voltage to provide a stable, perfectly matched output
Supporting MOSFETs (Metal Oxide Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistors) that act as microscopic, high-speed electronic regulators.
Inductors that temporarily store energy in a magnetic field and resist sudden changes in current to convert high-voltage wall electricity into a safe, steady flow for the battery
A remaining source of heat is the charging coil itself. To address that, Mophie’s engineers also designed a dedicated heat sink around the wireless coil that helps transfer heat away from the charging area where it can be passively dissipated through the integrated cooling vents.
The net effect is a charging architecture that promotes cooler operation by design rather than relying on fans or reducing charging speed after heat has already accumulated. This enables the chargers to maintain full 25W Qi2.2 charging performance while operating silently. During testing the phone never exceeded what might be described as a mildly warm temperature.
I told you it’s not simple engineering to move this clever stuff around, but we don’t need to worry about that. We can just benefit from the enhanced energy transfer efficiency.
Other 25W Qi2.2 wireless chargers will power up your iPhone at the same speed but a fan or StealthCharge makes it more likely that the 25W is passing without interruption.

				
					

				
			
Simon Jary




What does the Mophie Charge Stand include?
There are two versions of Mophie’s StealthCharge Charge Stands: a 3-in-1 model that wirelessly charges iPhone, Apple Watch, and AirPods; and a 4-in-1 model that adds a retractable USB-C cable for charging iPads and MacBooks.
All three or four devices can be charged at the same time, and Mophie includes a wall charger to cope with all the power: 40W for the 3-in-1 and a 100W charger for the 4-in-1.
Output:
Phone: 25W
AirPods: 5W
Apple Watch: 5W
Cable: 60W

				
					

				
			The 4-in-1 Charge Stand can charge an iPad, MacBook or a second iPhone (iPhone 15 or later) using its integrated retractable USB-C cable.
Simon Jary




The 25W iPhone-charging pad works with all iPhones from the iPhone 12 onwards, but only the iPhone 16 and later support the full 25W. iPhone 15 and earlier are limited to 15W charging.
In a stable charging environment 15W wireless charging should power an iPhone from 0% to 50% in around 45 minutes. 25W charging can do the same job in 30 minutes or so. The saved 15 minutes might not sound much and matters not at all if you are charging overnight but when you need power in a hurry it can make a noticeable difference.
The 5W Apple Watch charger is compliant with Apple’s Watch fast charge rules, and you can’t charge your AirPods any faster without inserting a cable.
The 4-in-1 Charge Stand’s retractable 2-foot (700mm) USB-C cable can supply up to 60W to a connected device, be that a second phone, an iPad, MacBook or other USB-C device.

				
					

				
			The 4-in-1 Mophie Charging Stand tucks a retractable 60W USB-C charging cable at the back of its base.
Simon Jary




60W will keep even the 16-inch MacBook Pro charged to a reasonable level and is not far off Apple’s fast-charge input (70W) for the MacBook Air and offers three times the power as the charger that Apple includes with its MacBook Neo (unless you are based in the U.K. or E.U. where Apple doesn’t include a charger at all!).
What’s it like to use every day?
The Mophie Charge Stand is a classic desk or nightstand elevated stand with the iPhone-charging pad at the front with a recessed AirPods case charging module below in the sturdy weighted base. The Apple Watch charger sticks out the back.
You can tilt the iPhone charging surface to your preferred viewing angle or flatten it straight for video chats.

				
					

				
			
Simon Jary




It has a small footprint, measuring 3.15 x 4.05 x 5.6 inches (80 x 103 x 142mm), and like most multi-device chargers will reduce messy and inconvenient cable clutter. It weighs 13.5oz (384g).
The lack of a fan makes this an appealing nightstand charger as you wouldn’t want a fan blaring away while you try to sleep. To be fair to charger manufacturers such as ESR (CryoBoost) and Belkin (ChillBoost), these fans are near whisper quiet. Read our reviews of the ESR CryoBoost 3-in-1 Magnetic 25W Charging Station and the Belkin UltraCharge Pro 3-in-1 Magnetic Charging Dock.
Each model is available in three colors: Black, White (silver with white charging areas) and Gold (closer to a champagne).

				
					

				
			
Simon Jary




How much does it cost?
The Mophie 3-in-1 Wireless Charge Stand is priced at $149.95 / £119.95. The 4-in-1 costs $179.95 / £139.95.
Mophie 3-in-1 Wireless Charge Stand
$149.95 at  Apple							
						
Mophie 4-in-1 Wireless Charge Stand
							

										
									Read our review
									
							
$179.95 at  Apple							
						
Both Charge Stands include a 1.5m USB-C cable. The 4-in-1 Charge Stand ships with a 100W wall charger, and the 3-in-1 a 40W charger.
Check out our reviews of the best wireless charging stations for alternatives, although if you admire this form factor there’s nothing really to stop you ordering one right away.

				
					

				
			
Simon Jary




Should you buy the Mophie Charge Stand?
The stand itself is a classic shape with the fastest-available wireless outputs in a choice of colors, and with a tilting iPhone charging pad. But the real benefit is in Mophie’s clever StealthCharge technology that promotes cooler charging by efficiently moving heat away from the device, delivering a cooler, faster, and quieter charging experience than found in most other Apple device chargers. The retractable cable in the 4-in-1 model means you shouldn’t need another charger even for your MacBook.

title: Apple Responds to Lawsuit Filed by Three YouTube Channels
content:
Earlier this year, three YouTube channels sued Apple, alleging that the company violated the U.S. Digital Millennium Copyright Act (DMCA) by unlawfully accessing and scraping millions of copyrighted YouTube videos to train its AI models.


In a class action lawsuit filed with the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California in April, the owners of the YouTube channels h3h3Productions, MrShortGame Golf, and Golfholics alleged that Apple "deliberately circumvented" YouTube's protections against video scraping and "profited substantially" by doing so.

Apple's actions were "not only unlawful, but an unconscionable attack on the community of content creators whose content is used to fuel the multi-trillion-dollar generative AI industry without any compensation," the complaint alleged.

h3h3Productions is a well-known YouTube channel created by Ethan Klein and Hila Klein, and they later created the H3 Podcast. Their channels have millions of followers, while MrShortGame Golf and Golfholics have hundreds of thousands of followers. The channels filed equivalent lawsuits against Meta, Nvidia, ByteDance, and Snap.

Apple Responds

Apple responded to the lawsuit this week, according to a court document viewed by MacRumors. In short, Apple said the plaintiffs made the videos publicly available on YouTube and that it was permitted to access the videos under the DMCA. Apple said YouTube's Terms of Service likewise permitted the company to access the videos.

"Plaintiffs allege that they posted audiovisual works to YouTube, and that any member of the public can see them there," reads Apple's response. "No password. No payment. No lock. No key. Allegedly, YouTube employs technological measures to prevent unauthorized downloading. But because YouTube provides public access to the videos, the alleged technological measures do not control access to the works, as § 1201(a) requires." 

Tags: Apple Lawsuits, YouTube
This article, "Apple Responds to Lawsuit Filed by Three YouTube Channels" first appeared on MacRumors.com

creator: Joe Rossignol

title: YouTuber Jon Prosser Responds to Apple's Lawsuit Over iOS 26 Leaks
content:
Jon Prosser today responded to Apple's lawsuit over iOS 26 leaks, with the YouTuber placing much of the blame on his acquaintance Michael Ramacciotti. 


As a refresher, Apple sued Prosser and Ramacciotti in July 2025 over alleged theft of the company's trade secrets. Apple alleged that the duo coordinated to break into former Apple software engineer Ethan Lipnik's development iPhone, in order to access and profit off details about "iOS 19," which ended up being called iOS 26.

Last year, Prosser leaked several details about "iOS 19" in videos shared on his YouTube channel Front Page Tech, several months before Apple announced the update at WWDC 2025. Later, an anonymous tipster informed Apple that the information was allegedly sourced from Lipnik's iPhone, which prompted the company to investigate.

Apple ultimately fired Lipnik and sued Prosser and Ramacciotti.

MacRumors, Prosser admitted to and denied many of Apple's claims, with some of the key ones summarized below:
Prosser denied that he planned or participated in any conspiracy or coordinated scheme with Ramacciotti for the purpose of injuring Apple.

Prosser admitted that he participated in a FaceTime video call with Ramacciotti, wherein Prosser was shown certain "iOS 19" features and apps running on the development iPhone.

Prosser denied that any of the information he viewed contained trade secrets.

Prosser denied knowing that the development iPhone belonged to Lipnik.

Prosser denied that he is in possession of any further confidential Apple information.
Prosser placed much of the blame on Ramacciotti. In the response, Prosser's attorney said that Ramacciotti's act of displaying the iOS 19 features on the FaceTime call was not induced by Prosser and, as such, Ramacciotti is "completely responsible" for the disclosure of Apple's alleged trade secrets, if any.

Prosser's attorney said Ramacciotti is responsible for all harm caused to Prosser.

Unfortunately, the attorney made several typos throughout the document, including writing that Prosser has "not knowledge" if iOS 19 was "unreleased." 

Prosser denies that he planned or participated in any conspiracy or coordinated scheme with Prosser Michael Ramacciotti ("Ramacciotti") for the purpose of injuring Apple. Any payments by Prosser were after the fact of the alleged theft by the former Apple employee, Michael Ramacciotti.
The case is before the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California. Prosser has requested a jury trial on all issues so triable by a jury.
Related Roundups: iOS 26, iPadOS 26
Tags: Jon Prosser, Apple Lawsuits
Related Forum: iOS 26

title: Top Stories: 'MacBook Ultra' and iPhone 18 Rumors, iOS 26.5.2 Security Fixes, and More
content:
After last week's craziness around Apple's major price hikes, things returned to a bit more of a normal flow this week, with the rumor mill offering up tidbits about the long-rumored touchscreen MacBook, the iPhone 18 lineup, and more.


This week also saw minor updates for iOS, iPadOS, and macOS that included a surprising number of security fixes, while a cyberattack at an Apple manufacturing partner has the company concerned about leaked confidential information, so read on below for all the details!

Top Stories

Apple's OLED 'MacBook Ultra' Will Stick With M5 Pro and M5 Max Chips

Last week, Bloomberg reported that Apple will be accelerating development of its M7 family of chips to deliver greater AI-focused capabilities sooner, but it also means Apple will not be releasing M6 Pro and M6 Max chips.


That revelation left open the question of which chips Apple is planning on using in its highly anticipated touchscreen "MacBook Ultra" rumored for release late this year or early next year, and Bloomberg now says Apple will be using M5 Pro and M5 Max chips for that product.

The news is likely to disappoint some who have been waiting for the new high-end MacBook, considering the M5 Pro and M5 Max have been used in the current MacBook Pro models since March, so the chips could be approaching a year old by the time the MacBook Ultra debuts.

check out our guide and recent video overview.

Bloomberg says there is an M6 model coming late this year, with a redesigned M7 model matching the upcoming MacBook Ultra to follow relatively soon after in the first half of 2027.

2027 iPhone 18 and iPhone 18e to Get 9GB RAM and A20 Chip

The lower-end iPhone 18 models set to launch in spring 2027 will feature 9GB DRAM, up from 8GB, according to Apple analyst Ming-Chi Kuo. By lower-end iPhones, Kuo is likely referencing the ‌iPhone 18‌ and the iPhone 18e, both of which are rumored to be coming around March or April of 2027.


The report is a bit of a surprise given previous rumors have indicated that at least the iPhone 18 would include 12GB of RAM, and a couple of iOS 27 features unveiled at WWDC last month are only available on iPhone and iPad devices with at least 12GB of RAM.

Apple 'Concerned' Over iPhone 18 Pro Data Leak From Supplier Tata

In late June, it was revealed that Apple manufacturing partner Tata had been the target of a cyberattack that resulted in confidential documents from Apple and other companies being leaked on the dark web.


Apple is unsurprisingly "concerned" about the leak from its Indian manufacturing partner, as the files include detailed information about parts and suppliers for the upcoming iPhone 18 Pro and iPhone 18 Pro Max.

crack down on distribution of the confidential content.

iPhone 18 Pro is Just a Few Months Away With These 10 New Features

Even aside from the Tata leak, a lot of information is already known about the iPhone 18 Pro and iPhone 18 Pro Max, which are expected to debut in September, so be sure to check out our overview.


Among the most recent leaks are an alleged SIM tray in a dark cherry color that has been rumored for the device, and an alleged diagram of the device's logic board showing a new layout for the A20 Pro chip with the RAM sitting next to the main processor rather than on top of it.

Apple could raise prices by up to $200 compared to the current iPhone 17 Pro lineup.

Apple Releases iOS 26.5.2 With Security Fixes

Apple this week released 26.5.2 updates for iOS, iPadOS, and macOS Tahoe, delivering fixes for more than 25 security vulnerabilities.


Apple says that it used the releases to deliver the included security fixes sooner than originally planned, as the company seeks to speed up fixes in an age where AI has made it easier and faster to exploit disclosed vulnerabilities.

Apple Acquires Award-Winning App 'Play'

In February, Apple notified the European Commission that it would be acquiring certain assets from and have the right to hire certain employees from Rabbit 3 Times, the company behind the award-winning app design tool Play. The notification was published on the European Commission's website this week, following a four-month waiting period.


Play was a Mac and iPhone app that allowed designers to prototype iPhone app interfaces using Apple's SwiftUI frameworks, and then send them to Xcode. In 2025, the app won an Apple Design Award for innovation, but it is no longer available in the App Store and it is unclear what Apple's plans are for the intellectual property associated with it.

MacRumors Newsletter

Each week, we publish an email newsletter like this highlighting the top Apple stories, making it a great way to get a bite-sized recap of the week hitting all of the major topics we've covered and tying together related stories for a big-picture view.

top stories like the above recap delivered to your email inbox each week, subscribe to our newsletter!

title: iOS 27 Public Beta Available This Month, Here's How to Get Your iPhone Ready Now
content:
Apple previously announced that the first iOS 27 public beta would be released in July, meaning that it should be available at some point this month.


Below, we have outlined how to get ready for the iOS 27 public beta, which will likely follow the third or fourth iOS 27 developer beta.

Release Date History

The first public betas of iOS 16 through iOS 26 came out between July 11 and July 24. 


  
iOS 26 Public Beta: Thursday, July 24, 2025

  
iOS 18 Public Beta: Monday, July 15, 2024

  
iOS 17 Public Beta: Wednesday, July 12, 2023

  
iOS 16 Public Beta: Monday, July 11, 2022



Get Ready

Once it is available, anyone will be able to install the iOS 27 public beta on a compatible iPhone for free by following the steps outlined below.

Sign up at beta.apple.com for free.
 
Open your iPhone's Settings app and tap General → Software Update → Beta Updates.

Select the iOS 27 Public Beta option (restart your iPhone if you don't see it) and follow the on-screen steps.
If you are impatient, anyone can install the iOS 27 developer beta for free right now.

Warning: While the first public beta is usually more stable than the first developer beta, iOS betas often have bugs and performance issues. You may not be able to use some apps that you rely on, and issues can extend to CarPlay. Backing up your iPhone before installing beta software is highly recommended, and relying on a secondary iPhone altogether is always a good idea if possible.

iOS 27 is compatible with the iPhone 11 and newer, but Apple Intelligence features like Siri AI are limited to the iPhone 15 Pro and newer.

has a waitlist. To join the waitlist, open the Settings app on iOS 27 and tap on Siri and you will find it there. In some cases, it can take a few weeks to receive access to Siri AI and the Siri app.

Related Roundups: iOS 27, iPadOS 27

title: New malicious clipboard clone raises serious security concerns for Mac users
content:
Macworld


Jamf Threat Labs has issued a report on new malware that users of the third-party clipboard manager Maccy need to be aware of. The malware, dubbed “PamStealer,” is distributed by malicious sites that impersonate the actual Maccy website, with downloadable files that trick visitors into thinking they are getting legitimate Maccy files.
The fake files are Maccy.scpt AppleScript files, made to look like legitimate installer files and distributed on disk images. If the script is launched, users are instructed to run the script, which then triggers the payload that can track information on your Mac and send it to a threat agent. The name PamStealer refers to the malware’s validation of the victim’s login password through the macOS Pluggable Authentication Modules (PAM).
To avoid downloading the malicious files, Maccy customers should make sure they are visiting the maccy.app website. According to a disclaimer on that website, “maccy.app is the only official website.” Customers can also visit the Maccy GitHub website at https://github.com/p0deje/Maccy, which states that “maccy.app is the only official website.”
Maccy is a free open-source clipboard manager that tracks clipboard history. Apple only just introduced a clipboard history tracker in macOS Tahoe through Spotlight, so these third-party managers are popular among power users. However, as Jamf explains, the delivery mechanism for this particular threat could have far-reaching implications beyond just this particular app:
Although disk images and AppleScript-based malware are well-established on macOS, PamStealer combines them in an interesting way. Rather than relying on shell commands such as curl or zsh, the AppleScript executes a self-contained JavaScript for Automation (JXA) downloader that retrieves and stages the payload using native Objective-C APIs. Combined with a Rust-based second stage and a password capture workflow that validates credentials locally through PAM, the result is a quieter execution chain than we typically observe in commodity macOS stealers.
The report goes into great depth on how the attack tricks users, and concludes: “Together, these behaviors illustrate how commodity macOS stealers continue to evolve, adopting quieter execution chains and native implementations that reduce traditional detection opportunities while remaining compatible with standard macOS features.”
How to protect yourself from malware
The easiest way to protect yourself from malware is to avoid downloading software from unfamiliar download sites. Never open links in emails or texts you receive from unknown and unexpected sources. If you get a message that looks like it is from an entity that you do business with, check the sender’s email address and inspect the URL carefully. If you see a link or button, you can Control-click it, select Copy Link Address, and then paste it into a text editor to see the actual URL to check it there.
Apple has vetted software in the Mac App Store, and it is the safest way to get apps. If you prefer not to patronize the Mac App Store, then buy software directly from the developer and their website. If you insist on using cracked software, you will always risk malware exposure.
Macworld has several guides to help, including a guide on whether or not you need antivirus software, a list of Mac viruses, malware, and trojans, and a comparison of Mac security software.
creator: Macworld

title: iPhone 18 Pro Could Use Qualcomm Modem in the US and C2 Elsewhere
content:
Stolen data from Apple manufacturing partner Tata Electronics appears to reveal that the iPhone 18 Pro will use different modem chips depending on the market it is sold in, with U.S. models retaining Qualcomm hardware while international models will feature Apple's in-house C2 modem.



wide-ranging cyberattack on Tata, which alongside Foxconn assembles the iPhone. More than 630GB of confidential data was stolen by a ransomware group calling itself "World Leaks" and has been circulating online. The material was obtained illegally and MacRumors has not seen the stolen files directly. AppleInsider conducted an analysis of the stolen files and said it could confirm the authenticity of several key documents.

iPhone Air, iPhone 17e, and M5 iPad Pro.

AppleInsider notes, is that the C2 still lacks mmWave capability, and that Apple is once again relying on Qualcomm to fill that gap for American carriers. 

mmWave is the ultra-high-frequency band of 5G offered primarily by Verizon, delivering very fast download speeds over short distances. Apple's C1 and C1X modems are widely regarded as more power efficient than their Qualcomm counterparts, meaning U.S. ‌iPhone 18 Pro‌ buyers may see somewhat worse battery life than those purchasing the same device elsewhere.

Daring Fireball's John Gruber offered analysis of the practical tradeoffs involved. While 5G outpaced LTE in his tests, Gruber argued the difference has no meaningful impact on how the phone actually feels to use:

Having a phone that can pull 320 Mbps down over cellular is like having a car that can go 320 MPH — an interesting technical feat, but of no practical value to me whatsoever. I never feel like I'm waiting for anything to load because I'm on LTE. LTE is fast enough, and regular 5G is more than fast enough. 5G mmWave is simply a waste of battery life as far as I'm concerned.


On why Apple would not simply deploy the C2 everywhere rather than retaining Qualcomm for the U.S. market, Gruber pointed the finger squarely at carrier economics:

Faster-than-you-practically-need download speeds are a carrier bragging point. Longer battery life and plenty-fast-enough download speeds are an Apple bragging point. Verizon — and to a lesser extent, AT&T — spent a fortune building out mmWave networks. They don't want to sell flagship phones that don't support them. Apple's flagship iPhones have supported those networks since 2020. If Zivkovic's analysis of this stolen data from Tata is correct, and Apple is going to use Qualcomm's modems only in iPhone 18 Pro models sold in the U.S., I think the reason why is Verizon and AT&T bragging points, not any practical user benefit. And the result may be that U.S. iPhone 18 Pro models get somewhat worse battery life than those in the rest of the world.


a rumored feature of the iPhone 18 Pro for years as part of Apple's broader effort to reduce its reliance on Qualcomm. A split deployment, with the C2 handling most of the world while Qualcomm covers the U.S., would represent a significant step in that direction even if it falls short of a complete transition.

title: Upgrade your MacBook with Satechi’s 5-in-1 USB-C hub for just $25
content:
Macworld


Satechi USB-C Pro Hub Adapter
View Deal
Satechi’s Type-C Pro Hub Adapter is on sale for $25 at Woot, a $55 savings that shaves off almost 70 percent off the usual price. This silver hub is built specifically for MacBooks, so it matches your machine and packs the ports Apple left out.
The port lineup is the best part about the hub because it transforms the two USB-C ports into a 4K HDMI output port, two USB 3.0 ports, a microSD card reader, and a Gigabit Ethernet port. That makes it possible to plug in a wired internet cable, a monitor, an extra keyboard, a mouse, and a memory card all at once.
The extra ports pull their weight, too. The Gigabit Ethernet port supports fast, stable wired internet, which can definitely save the day when Wi-Fi gets flaky. The USB-C Power Delivery lets you push up to 100W to your MacBook so you can charge your device. The two USB-A ports will make it possible to quickly transfer files at up to 5Gbps.
You do have to keep in mind that this hub’s HDMI output caps at 4K 30Hz, which is fine for spreadsheets, browsing, and presentations, but if you’re going to do any type of gaming, it might lag behind. The hub is specifically designed for the M1 and M2 MacBook Pro and MacBook Air, but it should work fine with any MacBook that has a pair of USB-C ports on onw side. It is not designed to work with single-USB-C-port models. 
At $25, this hub is a good pick if you want to make your MacBook more useful. Grab it while this Woot deal lasts, and keep in mind that if you have an Amazon Prime subscription, those free shipping perks extend to Woot.

title: MacPaw Moonlock review: Fast Mac antivirus that focuses on security
content:
Macworld


At a glance
Expert's Rating



Pros
Excellent malware detection
Fast scanning performance
Useful built-in VPN
Helpful security guidance
Clean, focused interface
Cons
Leaves some support files behind
No phishing website blocking
No scan time estimates
Trial requires payment information
Our Verdict 
MacPaw’s Moonlock succeeds by focusing on the security features that matter most instead of trying to become another bloated system cleaner. Its malware detection is fast and impressively thorough, the bundled VPN performs well for everyday travel use, and the educational Security Advisor encourages better online habits rather than overwhelming users with technical jargon. Malware cleanup occasionally leaves behind non-executable support files, and requiring payment information before the seven-day trial is disappointing, but overall Moonlock delivers an excellent balance of protection, usability, and value for home users and small offices.

Who it’s best for: Home users and small businesses wanting a focused, lightweight Mac security suite with excellent malware protection and a capable VPN.
Who it’s not for: Enterprise administrators or advanced users who require detailed logging, centralized management, or extensive network security controls.
At a glance
Protection: ★★★★★
Performance: ★★★★★
Features: ★★★★☆
Ease of use: ★★★★★
Value: ★★★★☆
Moonlock is MacPaw’s dedicated Mac security application, combining malware protection, VPN services, scam detection, and security education into a streamlined package. Rather than trying to replace every maintenance utility on your Mac, it concentrates on protecting your system against modern threats while encouraging safer computing habits.
For this review I tested Moonlock extensively under macOS Tahoe using more than 160 live malware samples from the Objective-See malware archive, evaluating its malware detection, VPN, scam detection, network protection, and overall performance. The results were consistently impressive, with only a handful of minor shortcomings.
Features: What do you get with MacPaw Moonlock?
Moonlock includes malware scanning, real-time protection, a VPN, Network Inspector, Scam Detector, System Protection, and Security Advisor. The suite focuses on practical security tools rather than unnecessary extras.
While it lacks parental controls, password management, and identity monitoring found in some competing suites, every included feature feels purposeful and well implemented.
Malware Protection

				
					

				
			
Customizing Moonlock’s Malware Scanner utility.

Foundry




Moonlock’s Malware Scanner is unquestionably the centerpiece of the suite. During initial testing it detected 266 malware components, integrating seamlessly with macOS XProtect and Gatekeeper under macOS Tahoe.
Real-time protection proved particularly effective. Compressed malware archives were detected successfully, including fake installers for popular applications such as Discord and WhatsApp. External USB drives are automatically scanned after mounting, and users can manually scan any folder or drive using the “Scan an Item…” feature.
Cleanup was excellent overall, although several malware families – including CrateDepression, DigitStealer, FlexibleFerret, Inqtana, and KandyKorn – left behind harmless support files after executable components had been removed.
Firewall & Network Protection

				
					

				
			
A Network Inspector tool can be used to block incoming traffic from specific countries.

Foundry




Rather than functioning as a traditional firewall, Moonlock’s Network Inspector lets users block traffic to and from selected countries. Regions can be filtered using predefined categories including Scam Risks, Sanctioned Countries, and Bot Traffic, making configuration straightforward while offering useful protection for many users.
Web & Phishing Protection
Moonlock’s Scam Detector uses AI to analyze images and up to 20,000 characters of text for phishing attempts and online scams. It produces reliable assessments with relatively few false positives.

				
					

				
			
The Scam Detector tool analyzes phishing text from an email.

Foundry




However, the suite does not actively block malicious websites or phishing URLs while browsing, leaving Safari and other browsers dependent upon their own built-in protections.
Extras (and What’s Missing)
The bundled VPN is surprisingly capable. While it lacks advanced administrative features such as activity logs or detailed configuration options, it delivers excellent performance, easy server switching, and includes a Kill Switch.
System Protection and Security Advisor also stand out by educating users rather than simply generating warnings. Helpful guidance, direct links into macOS Settings, and MacPaw’s entertaining “Cybersecuritoons” videos encourage better long-term security habits.
Test Results: How well does Moonlock protect your Mac?

				
					

				
			
Moonlock’s malware scanner removes dozens of quarantined items
.
Foundry




Moonlock successfully detected nearly every malware sample tested, including compressed archives and disguised application installers. Real-time protection reacted quickly and consistently throughout testing.
Real-World Testing
Testing consisted of more than 160 active and archived malware samples from the Objective-See security archive.
The scanner quarantined executable malware almost immediately while working smoothly alongside macOS’s own XProtect and Gatekeeper protections. Only a handful of non-executable support files remained after cleanup, leaving no functional malware behind.
External storage devices were scanned automatically upon connection, while manual scans of selected folders worked exactly as expected.
Reliability
Moonlock maintained an excellent balance between protection and usability. False positives were minimal, and security alerts were clear without becoming intrusive or overly aggressive.
Independent Lab Results
At the time of testing, recent independent AV-Test and AV-Comparatives Mac-specific benchmark results for Moonlock were not available. As a result, this review is based primarily on extensive hands-on testing using live malware samples under macOS Tahoe.
Methodology
Testing was performed on macOS Tahoe 26.5.1 using Moonlock’s complete feature set. More than 160 active and compressed malware samples from the Objective-See malware archive were evaluated alongside phishing detection, VPN performance, network controls, and everyday usability. Performance observations were made throughout normal multitasking to determine whether scanning noticeably impacted system responsiveness.
Performance: Will Moonlock slow down your Mac?
Moonlock proved exceptionally lightweight throughout testing. Full system scans completed quickly without noticeably affecting day-to-day work.
Animations remain smooth, interface responsiveness stays high, and background scanning rarely interferes with normal productivity.
Real-World Behaviour
Even while scanning hundreds of malware samples, my MacBook Pro remained responsive enough for everyday multitasking. The interface is polished, malware quarantine events are accompanied by satisfying sound effects, and the application’s visual design never comes at the expense of performance.
Key Metrics
While Moonlock currently doesn’t display scan duration or estimated completion times, overall scan speed was among the fastest observed during testing.
Ease of Use: Is Moonlock Mac-friendly?
Moonlock installs cleanly, requiring only Full Disk Access and Network Extension permissions before becoming fully operational.
Its interface feels modern and Mac-native, organizing features into clearly labelled modules without overwhelming users. Security Advisor and System Protection deserve particular praise for translating security recommendations into actionable steps, often linking directly to the appropriate macOS Settings pages instead of forcing users to hunt through menus.
Moonlock pricing and plans 
Moonlock is competitively priced for individual Mac users and small offices. Subscription plans offer the best long-term value, while perpetual licenses remain available for those who prefer one-time purchases.

MacsOne-Time PurchaseAnnual SubscriptionMonthly

1 Mac$150$54/year ($4.50/month)$13.50/month
2 Macs$240$84/year ($7/month)

5 Macs$480$168/year ($14/month)






Moonlock offers strong value thanks to its excellent malware protection, capable VPN, and thoughtful security education features. While it lacks some enterprise capabilities and advanced extras found in larger security suites, its focused approach makes it an appealing alternative for users who simply want dependable Mac protection without unnecessary complexity.
Should you buy MacPaw Moonlock?
Yes. Moonlock is one of MacPaw’s strongest products to date because it understands exactly what it wants to be: a focused Mac security suite rather than another all-purpose maintenance utility. Malware protection is excellent, the VPN performs surprisingly well, and the educational tools genuinely help users improve their security habits. Requiring payment details before beginning the free trial is unfortunate, and enterprise users may want more advanced management tools, but for home users and small offices Moonlock is an easy recommendation and one of the better Mac security products currently available.

title: Report reveals iPhone 18 Pro Max’s ‘exact’ battery capacity—and it’s huge
content:
Macworld


It may not be regarded as the flagship of the iPhone range as of this fall (that honor will transfer to the folding iPhone Ultra), but this year’s Pro Max model will have at least something to boast about: one of the biggest batteries ever to grace an iPhone. That’s if a new report is accurate, at any rate.
According to a leak on social media this week, the iPhone 18 Pro Max will have a battery capacity of 5,235 mAh on the Nano SIM model and a whopping 5,425 mAh on the eSIM-only version. That’s an increase of 8.5 percent and 6.6 percent, respectively, compared to the 4,823 mAh/5,088 mAh capacities of the iPhone 17 Pro Max models.
Those are solid year-over-year bumps, though not among the biggest we’ve seen. Last month, we heard that the iPhone 18 Pro will only go up by 1.5 percent and 0.8 percent across the two models, compared to previous annual increases of up to 19 percent.
But in absolute terms, compared with phones outside the Apple bubble, these would be impressive figures. In the past, Apple’s phones were often criticized for their relatively small batteries, but 5,425 mAh would stand comparison with most rival offerings. The Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra, for example, has a capacity of 5,000 mAh.
iPhone battery capacities have been rising steadily for the past few generations. Here’s how the rumored figures compare with the previous five Pro Max models:
iPhone 13 Pro Max: 4,352 mAh
iPhone 14 Pro Max: 4,323 mAh
iPhone 15 Pro Max: 4,441 mAh
iPhone 16 Pro Max: 4,685 mAh
iPhone 17 Pro Max: 4,823 mAh (Nano SIM), 5,088 mAh (eSIM only)
iPhone 18 Pro Max: 5,235 mAh (Nano SIM, claimed), 5,425 mAh (eSIM only, claimed)
Is this report legitimate?
The key question about all this is, as with most iPhone rumors, whether this report can be trusted. That’s debatable. Even tracing its origin is complicated.
We first saw it covered by the reputable news site GSMArena, which linked to a since-deleted tweet by a leaker going by the handle @fireuniverse8. However, we’ve found this still-available tweet, which appears to contain largely the same information: Two batteries that seemingly belong to the iPhone 18 Pro Max, labeled with corresponding battery capacities.
With us so far? Good.
We can’t see the image from the original tweet, which may have named a source in the form of a watermark or similar. But we can still see the accompanying text: “Even iPhone 18 Pro Max battery higher than S26 Ultra,” which matches up with the numbers in a general sense. But that doesn’t give us much to go on, or reason to believe it’s legitimate.
The remaining tweet, meanwhile, contains a screenshot of a post on Weibo that appears to come from an entirely different person. Using Google Translate, their account name comes up as “Doge Internet Technology,” and the avatar is different. Is this the original source? Or, as seems more likely, is this a follower of Fire Universe discussing the information disclosed in the previous tweet, and Fire Universe then reposting that?

				
					

				
			FireUniverse8
(If you’re interested, Doge’s commentary reads: “The world has suffered from SIM cards for too long. iPhone 18 Pro Max Battery. Compared to the overseas eSIM version, it has 190 mAh less battery life. What will it use to compensate?”)
Fire Universe isn’t a leaker we’re familiar with, so we don’t have a track record to check. So what that leaves us with is a leaker without much of a reputation posting a rumor without a source, or repeating a rumor from another unfamiliar leaker, then deleting the tweet, but leaving up a tweet where we can still see the numbers. Hopefully that’s all clear.
Under normal circumstances, we’d advise extreme skepticism with something like this, but the one point in its favor is the fact that other sources have previously predicted something similar. In February, another Weibo leaker (one who is more familiar to the rumorsphere, Digital Chat Station) claimed the 18 Pro Max would have battery capacities of roughly 5000mAh and 5100-5200mAh, respectively. The latest numbers are more optimistic, but largely align with the original claim.
One final note: there’s a reason why I said one of the biggest batteries ever to grace an iPhone. The iPhone Ultra is expected to have a battery capacity of around 5,500mAh, and could go as high as 5,800 mAh. That would be really big and one of the largest ever—the Galaxy Z Fold 7, for example, only uses a 4,400mAh battery, and the latest iPad mini has a 5,078 mAh battery. But that’s why it’s going to be the flagship–and why it could cost as much as $2,500.
creator: Macworld

title: New Mac infostealer confirms stolen passwords before stealing data
content:
A newly discovered macOS infostealer verifies Mac login passwords before stealing sensitive data, giving attackers immediate confirmation that compromised credentials will actually work.

PamStealer
Researchers at Jamf Threat Labs have documented a new macOS malware campaign built around an infostealer called PamStealer. PamStealer disguises itself as the Maccy clipboard manager and uses AppleScript alongside a Rust payload to infect Macs.
Jamf found that PamStealer verifies login passwords through Apple's Pluggable Authentication Modules before stealing additional data. Password verification sets PamStealer apart from most macOS infostealers, which typically capture whatever password a victim enters without confirming that it's valid.
The campaign begins with a fake website that closely imitates the legitimate Maccy clipboard manager. Next, the fake website delivers a malicious AppleScript application disguised as Maccy.

 Continue Reading on AppleInsider | Discuss on our Forums
creator: Andrew Orr <andreworr@appleinsider.com>

title: Opera Browser Gains Protection Against Malicious Clipboard Commands
content:
Opera browser has announced a new security feature called Paste Protect that aims to stop clipboard-based cyberattacks before their malicious commands can be accidentally executed.


Opera says it's the first major browser to offer native protection against ClickFix attacks – a growing form of social engineering that tricks users into copying and pasting malicious commands into a computer's terminal. The new feature is built into Opera's desktop browsers and enabled by default. 

ClickFix attacks typically masquerade as routine troubleshooting prompts, such as fake CAPTCHA verification or video playback fixes. Once pasted and executed, the commands can install malware, steal passwords, or give attackers remote access to a device. Opera describes the browsing risk as follows:
A ClickFix-style attack usually starts with something small and ordinary: a video that won't play, or a CAPTCHA that won't quite verify you're human. A pop-up offers a fix, telling you to copy a short command and paste it into your computer's terminal. It looks like routine troubleshooting. In reality, that command can install malware, steal saved passwords, or hand an attacker remote access to your machine, all carried out by the user's own hands, on their own device.
Opera features an existing clipboard hijack protection feature that prevents external applications from silently replacing copied content such as cryptocurrency wallet addresses. Paste Protect combines this with a new injection protection system that monitors clipboard activity for suspicious commands copied from websites and blocks potentially malicious content before it reaches the clipboard. 

Users can see the first 120 characters of the blocked content, and developers working with trusted sources can override the block or mark specific sites as safe.

Huntress that said ClickFix accounted for more than 53 percent of malware-loading cyberattacks last year, indicating the rapid growth of the technique. 

introduced a related safeguard for the Mac with the release of macOS Tahoe 26.4 earlier this year. Following the update, the operating system explicitly warns the user before they paste potentially dangerous commands into the Terminal app. 

company's website.
Tag: Opera Browser
This article, "Opera Browser Gains Protection Against Malicious Clipboard Commands" first appeared on MacRumors.com

title: Bitdefender Antivirus for Mac review: Excellent protection held back by frustrating flaws
content:
Macworld


At a Glance
Expert's Rating



Pros
Excellent malware detection
Fast scan performance
Very low system impact
Effective ransomware protection
Generous 30-day free trial
Cons
InConsistent phishing protection
Chat Protection needs improvement
No scheduled scans
Log export unavailable
VPN requires separate subscription
Our Verdict 
Bitdefender Antivirus for Mac remains one of the stronger security suites available for macOS, combining excellent malware detection, fast scan performance, and minimal system impact in an easy-to-use package. Standout features such as Safe Files ransomware protection and the integrated VPN add genuine value, while newer additions like Chat Protection and Web Protection still need refinement. Although the lack of scheduled scans and log exporting may frustrate advanced users and IT administrators, Bitdefender continues to offer outstanding protection at a competitive price.

Who it’s best for: Mac users who want strong, lightweight antivirus protection with excellent malware detection and minimal performance impact.
Who it’s not for: Users who require advanced administrative features, scheduled scanning, or a fully featured VPN without paying for an additional subscription.
At a glance
Protection: ★★★★☆
Performance: ★★★★★
Features: ★★★★☆
Ease of use: ★★★★☆
Value: ★★★★☆
Bitdefender Antivirus for Mac is one of the best-known cross-platform security suites available for Mac users. Rather than attempting to reinvent antivirus software, the latest version focuses on refining its already impressive protection while introducing AI-powered additions such as Chat Protection.
For this review, Bitdefender Antivirus for Mac version 10.3.2.2 was tested under macOS Tahoe 26.5.1 using more than 150 malware samples alongside phishing, browser security, and ransomware testing. While the core antivirus engine remains excellent, several newer features still need polish before matching the quality of Bitdefender’s traditional protection.
Features: What do you get with Bitdefender?
Bitdefender bundles together antivirus protection, ransomware defence, browser security, Safe Files, VPN functionality, and the new AI-powered Chat Protection module. Installation remains straightforward, requiring Full Disk Access and network extension permissions before becoming operational.
The package focuses on protection first, while several premium features – including unlimited VPN usage – remain optional paid upgrades.
Malware Protection

				
					

				
			
Bitdefender’s Protection tab provides access to its security features
.
Foundry




Real-time protection remains Bitdefender’s greatest strength. Both Quick Scan and System Scan completed impressively quickly while detecting over 95% of more than 150 malware samples during testing. Compressed archives are scanned automatically, and detected threats are quarantined without interrupting normal workflow.
Like several competitors, Bitdefender occasionally relied on macOS XProtect and Gatekeeper to finish blocking certain developer files associated with malware. Fake versions of Transmission, WhatsApp and Discord installers were allowed onto the system before macOS ultimately prevented execution. Several developer scripts—including Flasfa.py, GetShell.sh, Invisibleferret.py, PokerStealer.sh and others—were also permitted to launch before Apple’s own protections intervened.
While these cases were limited, users would benefit from clearer notifications explaining exactly when Bitdefender has blocked a threat.
Firewall & Network Protection
Bitdefender does not include a traditional configurable firewall for macOS, instead relying on Apple’s built-in firewall while expanding network protection through its VPN service.
The VPN supports WireGuard, OpenVPN UDP and OpenVPN TCP alongside automatic protocol selection, automatic activation on unsecured Wi-Fi, an Internet Kill Switch, and automatic VPN activation when peer-to-peer applications launch.
Although not as comprehensive as dedicated VPN products, it’s one of the better bundled VPN implementations available.
Web & Phishing Protection
Web Protection is inconsistent.
TrafficLight installs easily across Safari, Firefox and Chrome, but browser performance varies considerably.
Safari performed poorly during testing, allowing nearly every phishing and scam link contained within Gmail’s Spam folder. Firefox blocked some phishing attempts but still permitted access to questionable clone websites. Chrome delivered the strongest overall performance but continued to miss several phishing pages.
The browser support is welcome, but detection consistency still trails the quality of Bitdefender’s malware engine.
Extras (and What’s Missing)

				
					

				
			
Customizing protected folders with Bitdefender’s “Safe Files” feature
.
Foundry




Safe Files remains one of Bitdefender’s standout features.
Desktop, Documents, Downloads and Pictures folders receive ransomware protection by default, while additional folders can easily be added. Blacklists and whitelists provide fine-grained control over which applications may modify protected content.
The new AI-powered Chat Protection successfully identifies obvious scams but currently struggles with more sophisticated social engineering attempts.
Missing features remain equally noticeable. Scheduled scans are unavailable, VPN functionality requires a separate subscription for unlimited access, and log exporting remains absent.
Test Results: How well does Bitdefender protect your Mac?
Bitdefender delivered excellent malware detection throughout testing, successfully identifying more than 95% of malware samples while maintaining extremely fast scanning performance.
Real-World Testing

				
					

				
			
A Bitdefender quick scan in progress
.
Foundry




Testing consisted of more than 150 malware samples sourced from the Objective-See malware archive.
Quick Scan completed in just 4 minutes 29 seconds, while a complete System Scan covering over 800GB of data finished in 47 minutes 8 seconds.
Bitdefender detected malware during scanning, quarantining threats automatically while working alongside macOS XProtect and Gatekeeper to eliminate active components.
Developer scripts represented the primary weakness, with several samples launching before Apple’s own protections completed the blocking process.
Web Protection proved considerably less reliable, with browser performance varying significantly between Safari, Firefox and Chrome.
Reliability
Bitdefender proved stable throughout testing and never crashed under macOS Tahoe 26.5.1.
False positives remained minimal, although notification behaviour could be improved by more clearly informing users whenever malware has been actively blocked.
Independent Lab Results
Bitdefender consistently performs well in independent testing from organisations including AV-Test and AV-Comparatives, regularly earning top marks for malware detection while maintaining low false positive rates. Our hands-on testing closely reflected those results, particularly regarding malware detection speed and overall performance.
Bitdefender got full marks in the March 2026 AV-Test results:
Protection: 6/6
Performance: 6/6
Usability: 6/6
Total: 18/18
The latest AV-Comparatives Mac Security Test (published 17 June 2026) also certified Bitdefender after testing it on macOS Tahoe. It achieved 100% malware detection and received AV-Comparatives’ Approved Security Product certification.
Methodology
Our testing was performed using macOS Tahoe 26.5.1 on a Mac equipped with a 1TB SSD containing more than 800GB of data.
Evaluation combined real-world malware testing using more than 150 Objective-See malware samples alongside phishing testing, ransomware protection evaluation, browser security testing, application stability, performance measurements, and overall usability.
Performance: Will Bitdefender slow down your Mac?
Performance remains one of Bitdefender’s biggest strengths.
Despite scanning a drive containing over 800GB of data, both Quick Scan and System Scan completed rapidly while having very little impact on normal system responsiveness.
Real-World Behaviour
Background protection remained largely invisible throughout testing.
Applications launched normally, multitasking performance stayed responsive, and no crashes occurred. Both installation and uninstallation completed without issue.
Ease of Use: Is Bitdefender Mac-friendly?
Installation remains straightforward, with Bitdefender requesting only the permissions necessary for operation before becoming fully functional.
The interface is clean, modern, and easy to navigate without feeling like a Windows application transplanted onto macOS. Individual security modules are clearly organised, Safe Files is especially easy to configure, and notifications remain relatively unobtrusive.
Documentation is comprehensive, although the support experience would benefit from integrated tutorial videos alongside the existing manuals.
Bitdefender pricing and plans
Bitdefender remains competitively priced, particularly during promotional periods, although unlimited VPN access requires an additional subscription.

DevicesOne-YearTwo-YearThree-Year

1 Device$24.99 (normally $49.99)$69.99 $89.99
3 Devices$24.99 (normally $69.99)$89.99$119.99





The 30-day trial remains one of Bitdefender’s biggest advantages, requiring only an email address and no payment details.
The core antivirus protection easily justifies the asking price, although the additional VPN subscription slightly reduces the overall value proposition compared to suites that bundle unlimited VPN access.
Should you buy Bitdefender Antivirus for Mac?
Yes – provided your priorities are excellent malware protection and outstanding performance.
Bitdefender Antivirus for Mac continues to deliver some of the strongest malware detection available for macOS while remaining lightweight enough to leave running continuously. Although Web Protection and the new Chat Protection feature still need refinement, the core antivirus engine remains exceptionally strong. Users looking for scheduled scans, enterprise logging features, or unlimited VPN access may find better value elsewhere, but for most Mac owners Bitdefender remains one of the safest recommendations in the consumer antivirus market.

title: Amazon forgot to raise M4 iPad Air prices–grab one for $200 off right now
content:
Macworld


Apple iPad Air M4
View Deal
Apple raised iPad Air prices by $150 last week, but over at Amazon you can still get one for even less than the old price. You can grab Apple’s 11-inch M4 iPad Air for $559 or the 13-inch M4 iPad Air for just $749 right now. That’s not quite as low as Prime Day’s $80-$100 savings, but that was before Apple raised prices. So, now you’re saving $200 off the new MSRP and $40-$50 off the older price.
This is a powerful tablet that you’ll get to take with you everywhere. The M4 chip delivers advanced graphics and a snappy performance that’s fantastic for multitasking. Whether you’re bouncing between apps or editing photos, this tablet will perform just fine. The chip also supports Apple Intelligence, so you’ll have tons of fun with those features too, including the new Siri Ai coming this fall with iPad OS 27.
The tablet comes with 128GB of storage space, which isn’t a ton, but it’s a decent amount of room for apps, games, and whatever else you need to store on there. The display is as gorgeous as ever, as the 11-inch or 13-inch Liquid Retina screen delivers vivid colors, great contrast, and super low reflectivity. Pair that with the excellent all-day battery, and you have a device you can use for work, doomscrolling, gaming, and whatever else you feel like around the block.
If you want to take things further and make this into a true productivity tool, you’ll need to pay a bit extra for an Apple Pencil Pro and the Magic Keyboard, both of which are sold separately.
The bottom line is that you can save $200 on an Apple iPad Air with a speedy M4 chip, and that’s a smart choice.

title: Popular Show Tracking App TV Time Shutting Down on July 15
content:
TV Time, the popular show and movie tracking app, is shutting down on July 15, with all personal user data set to be deleted after that date.


In a support page update announcing the news, the company admitted that it was "no longer sustainable to continue operating the service as a free app," and said that there was "not enough demand for a paid app."

Come the shutdown date, the TV Time app will be removed from both the App Store and Google Play, and the tvtime.com website will go offline permanently.

GDPR self-service tool before the July 15 cutoff. The company says all personal user data will be deleted after that date, but it may retain aggregated, non-personal data for business or legal purposes.

Resetera forums have suggested alternatives like Trakt, Serializd, and Simkl – although the latter's servers have reportedly struggled under a sudden wave of new sign-ups.

title: macOS 27 Golden Gate marks the end of built-in DVD support
content:
Macworld


If you’re still using DVD media on a Mac with the most current operating system, you’ll need to make arrangements soon to continue using those discs. As noted in the macOS 27 Golden Gate beta 2 release notes, the DVDPlayback framework is not in the macOS 27 Golden Gate SDK, which means that apps can no longer use it. The notes go on to say that “in a future macOS release,” Apple will remove the framework completely.
That leaves the door open for the built-in DVD Player app to be included in macOS 27 Golden Gate when it is officially released this fall. This also likely means that Golden Gate will be the last version of macOS to include a native DVD app. Users will eventually have to turn to a third-party app, such as VLC.
The DVDPlayback framework is quite old and Apple has not updated it in years. The neglect shows–DVD Player produces playback that is jerky and of low image quality. I’ve had much better results using VLC. Apple doesn’t even make the DVD Player app easily findable; it’s located in the /System/Library/CoreServices/Applications folder, not in the regular Applications folder. The DVD Player app automatically launches if you insert a DVD into a connected DVD drive.

title: Apple has all the tech needed for great Mac games. So where are they?
content:
Macworld


I’ve been a computer enthusiast for as long as I can remember, and that means I’ve sat on both sides of the Mac/PC aisle in my time. And in fact, right now I have both a MacBook Pro and a custom gaming PC sitting on my desk.
There’s a good reason for that. I love playing games, and right now Apple simply can’t match the breadth of games available on Windows. If I want to have the most choice available for my gaming downtime—and I do—then there’s very little choice but to have a Windows machine close at hand.
It really feels like the situation shouldn’t be this way. macOS has come on leaps and bounds in recent years with its support for cutting-edge gaming technology, including features like upscaling, ray tracing, and frame generation that can make a huge difference to the user experience.
The hardware, too, is more capable than it’s ever been. If you’d told me a few years ago that I’d be able to play a game as notoriously demanding as Cyberpunk 2077 on a Mac, I’d have laughed you out of the building. Yet here we are.
But for all that progress, there’s still a major dearth of AAA games on the Mac. It’s the number one reason I haven’t ditched my Windows PC yet, and I’m sure I’m not the only one. I can’t help but wonder: if things still aren’t improving on this front, are we ever going to see parity between macOS and Windows in terms of available games? And what is Apple doing to put things right?
A barren gaming landscape
Recently, I’ve been playing a lot of Crimson Desert. It’s a massive open world fantasy adventure in the vein of popular titles like Skyrim and The Witcher 3, and unlike most highly ambitious games of its ilk, it was available on macOS on day one, at the same time as its PC and PlayStation 5 launch.

				
					

				
			Crimson Desert is the rare example of a top-tier game landing on Mac simultaneously with Windows.
Foundry




Yet situations like this are few and far between. For Mac gamers, the gaming landscape really is barren compared to what you get on Windows.
Sure, there are plenty of big-name draws like Baldur’s Gate 3, Assassin’s Creed Shadows and Cyberpunk 2077 on the Mac. But the list of prominent absentees is long and noteworthy, with titles like Call of Duty, Diablo IV, Elden Ring, Arc Raiders and more nowhere to be found. Past entries in popular series like Assassin’s Creed are unavailable and many of the best Mac games—names like Control Ultimate Edition and Disco Elysium—are either years old or debuted on Windows long before they came to the Mac.
There’s no sign that that’s about to change. The fact that Crimson Desert arrived on macOS at the same time as on Windows was surprising. If anything, it was the exception that proved the rule: most games come to Mac late, if they arrive at all. The fact that something as high-profile as Crimson Desert bucked the trend demonstrates how strong that trend really is.
It’s only in recent years that Apple has made a concerted effort to woo both gamers and game developers, and it’ll take time for any changes to be felt. But if things are going to improve, Apple can’t afford to let up.
Limited progress
Historically, Mac gaming always faced a chicken-and-egg problem. Developers didn’t want to make games for macOS because there weren’t many Mac players. And gamers didn’t want to switch to macOS because there’s weren’t many games available. This vicious cycle saw each side exacerbate the other.
Apple, to its credit, has tried to rectify the situation. On the software side, for example, it’s worked directly with developers to optimize their games for macOS and learn how the company can make the macOS development landscape more appealing.
As for hardware, Apple silicon has brought about a step change in the gaming capabilities of the Mac. While it’s not as good as having a discrete graphics card like an Nvidia RTX 5090, of course, it’s made gaming a realistic prospect for most of Apple’s computers.
And then there are techs like MetalFX Upscaling and MetalFX Frame Interpolation, which make gaming possible on Macs that never would have stood a chance before.
MetalFX Upscaling takes a low-resolution frame and scales it up to a higher resolution, bringing more detail and clarity to your gaming sessions. So, your Mac might render a frame at roughly 1080p, then MetalFX steps in to scale that up to 4K or thereabouts. The key here is that your Mac doesn’t have to actually draw a 4K frame each time, saving resources and increasing frame rates as a result. The Windows equivalents are DLSS for Nvidia graphics cards and FSR for AMD cards.

				
					

				
			A game like Control: Ultimate Edition wouldn’t run very well without MetalFX upscaling.
Remedy




MetalFX Frame Interpolation, meanwhile, is Apple’s equivalent of the frame-generation tech used in PC graphics cards made by the likes of Nvidia and AMD. In short, it uses AI to generate one additional frame for every two existing frames. The effect of this is smoother gameplay than you would normally expect from your hardware.
Combined, these technologies mean you can get an enjoyable gaming experience on a relatively low-spec Mac – provided game developers actually implement these features, of course.
But even when that’s the case, you’ll still get the nagging feeling—borne out by benchmarks and developer guidance, unfortunately—that performance is still better on other, non-Apple systems. According to Crimson Desert’s creator Pearl Abyss, you’ll need an M2 Pro chip from 2023 at a minimum if you want to hit 60fps at a paltry 720p resolution. Windows users only need an Nvidia RTX 2080 for the same frame rate, at a higher 1080p resolution. That card was released all the way back in 2018.
In other words, you’ll need a newer chip and the higher associated costs to get a lower resolution output on a Mac compared to a Windows PC. That makes for painful reading.
Benchmarking from content creator Andrew Tsai, meanwhile, showed similarly disappointing results. Running Crimson Desert on a MacBook Pro with the M5 Max chip saw average frame rates around 60fps on medium settings at 1440p, although that was using MetalFX Upscaling to increase the resolution up from 960p. Bear in mind that such a laptop will cost you a shocking $4,099 or more thanks to Apple’s recent price increases.
After setting my personal gaming PC to a native 1440p resolution (not upscaled) and maximum settings in Crimson Desert, I got around 50fps, meaning it looked far better than the Mac with similar frame rates. To mirror the benchmarked MacBook Pro, I dropped my PC to medium settings and 900p upscaled resolution with the DLSS Balanced preset, at which point it got to around 110fps. That’s almost double the performance of the M5 Max MacBook Pro, despite my PC costing over $1,000 less.

				
					

				
			Do you want to pay more to run Baldur’s Gate 3 at half the frame rate? 
Larian Studios




As long as you get incredibly worse performance on a Mac that costs a lot more, gamers are likely to stay away. And that will only discourage developers from releasing their products on Apple’s platforms. Better performance at every price point, relative to competing Windows computers, is therefore an important sticking point that Apple needs to address.
What more can Apple do?
We’re starting to see the fruits of Apple’s labor as more big-name developers bring their games to macOS. But clearly, more needs to be done. Until players no longer feel like they’re having to constrain their game library or stymie their performance by going with an Apple device, Mac gaming will always be at a disadvantage. Apple needs to continue working with developers to convince them to bring their games to macOS and to do it on day one, with no delay.
At the same time, Apple must keep improving its hardware and software capabilities to ramp up gaming performance year over year. It’s not reasonable to expect people to pay over $4,000 to get middling gaming performance that’s half of a Windows PC that costs considerably less.
Apple is working on these two approaches, and they’re starting to make a difference. But it’s far too soon to stop now.

				
					

				
			
The newest MacBook Pro is powerful enough to play most of the games my PC can, but they’re just not available.

Foundry




Aside from that, Apple could do with making itself more visible to gamers. I’m not expecting it to be the marquee sponsor of The Game Awards, but teaming up with a big-name gaming brand for a range of exclusive products would raise its profile. Apple has already done this with Corsair (with the K65 Plus gaming keyboard) so we know it’s possible.
Taking the same approach that Apple has done with Apple TV might also yield results. There, Apple has poured money into its own streaming service and secured prominent exclusives to tempt customers. The company made a half-hearted attempt to do something similar with Apple Arcade, but that never really felt like much of a priority, and these days it’s a sad shadow of its promised potential. Working with prominent development studios and publishers to launch Mac-exclusive games that people actually want to play is something I’d love to see.
Mac gaming used to be a joke, especially in the bad old days of Intel chips’ integrated graphics. Things are looking brighter these days, but there’s clearly a long way to go before Mac gamers can stand shoulder to shoulder with their Windows counterparts.
I’m hopeful that one day, this continued progress will mean I can finally ditch my Windows PC and go all-in on macOS. Right now, though, gaming is the one thing holding me back.
creator: Macworld

title: Apple Ramps Foldable iPhone 'Ultra' Production to 10 Million Units
content:
Apple has told suppliers to prepare to make approximately 10 million foldable iPhones this year, up from a previous forecast of about 7-8 million units a few months ago, reports Nikkei Asia ($).


Apple has already booked parts for roughly 80 million smartphones for the second half of 2026, which includes the iPhone 18 Pro, iPhone 18 Pro Max, and the first-ever foldable iPhone. The company's full 2026 production is expected to top 220 million units, according to the publication.

Apple's purchasing power is said to have left it better positioned than rivals like Xiaomi, Oppo, and Vivo, which have each cut annual production targets below 100 million units amid an industry-wide memory shortage.

Some suppliers have reportedly been told to expect orders for as many as 85 million new iPhones in the second half of 2026, with Apple asking them to reserve iPhone 17 components for the coming iPhone 18 lineup.

appear to have been resolved, but that has raised the odds of a small initial shipment following the device's launch. A larger production run likely won't begin until closer to the end of the year.

raised prices on MacBooks and iPads last month in response to rising component costs, but the iPhone 17 lineup has so far been spared from a price hike. If that remains the case, Apple will likely use the new devices launches to introduce increased pricing across the lineup. IDC has predicted that the foldable will carry an average selling price of $2,500, with storage options potentially priced as high as $3,000.

title: Steve Jobs' Bicentennial check captures the birth of Apple and the PC revolution
content:

RR Auction is currently running a Fine Autographs and Artifacts auction, full of signatures from American presidents, political figures, authors, kings, queens, scientists, engineers, innovators, and everyone in between. Among the famous names is Steve Jobs, Apple's co-founder and former CEO.
It's hardly the first time Jobs-signed item has made it to the auction block, but this one comes with remarkable timing. The $10 payment to the People's Computer Company (PCC) is dated July 4, 1976, America's Bicentennial, just months after Apple was founded.

title: Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 Available Again After U.S. Lifts Export Controls
content:
Anthropic's Fable 5 model is once again available for use, the company said today. Claude users are now seeing the option to use Fable 5, with Anthropic rolling out an in-app message.


Through July 7, eligible Claude subscribers can use up to 50 percent of their plan's weekly usage limit on Fable 5. After hitting that limit, Fable 5 use will require credits. After July 7, Fable will be available through usage credits.

first came out on June 9. Fable 5's capabilities exceed those of any model it has made generally available, and it has demonstrated "exceptional performance" for software engineering, knowledge work, vision, scientific research, and more. It outperforms Opus models on longer, more complex tasks. Fable 5 can work autonomously for longer than any prior Claude model.

Though Anthropic released Fable 5 with conservative safeguards to prevent misuse, the Trump administration applied export controls to the model, forcing Anthropic to restrict access to foreign nationals. Anthropic had no way of verifying the nationality of people using its models, so it had to suspend access to Fable 5. At the same time, Anthropic also had to restrict access to Mythos 5, the next model in its Project Glasswing initiative for major companies and federal agencies seeking help defending critical infrastructure.

The order came after Amazon researchers found a prompt able to bypass Fable's safeguards, and the model found software vulnerabilities. Anthropic investigated and discovered that older models and models from competing companies could also locate the same vulnerabilities. Anthropic ended up shipping a new classifier that blocks the technique in more than 99 percent of cases.

Fable 5 is available for Pro, Max, Team, and select Enterprise plans. Anthropic has also restored Mythos 5 access for U.S. organizations that are part of Project Glasswing.

Tag: Anthropic
